text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Team GB's runners will be pounding a bright lavender circuit containing seashells - here's why

TheParis 2024 Olympicshave almost arrived. TheOpening Ceremonywill take place this evening, with diverTom Daleyand rowerHelen Gloverthe flag bearers for Team GB.

The British team will be represented by 327 athletes, across a range of disciplines, all hoping to bring home a coveted medal.

Competitors from England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland are all taking part.

The athletics contingent this year includesDina Asher-Smith,Katarina Johnson-Thompson, Sam Atkin, Jeremiah Azu and Emile Cairess.

Asher-Smith is looking to become the first British woman to claim Olympic sprint gold in either the 100m or 200m, while marathon runner Caireses finished third at this year’sLondon Marathonin a remarkable time of just 2:06:46, making him the second-fastest British marathoner ever.

Speaking of her ambitions at this year’s games, Asher-Smith seems confident. “I’m running great,” she said. “I’m happy that as we’re coming into the crunch time in the season everything’s coming together. I’m on track.

“I want to be so successful, I want to do myself proud and I want to create history. It’s not my first rodeo and I’m very excited going into my third Olympic Games in a really good place.”

Viewers may be used to seeing Olympic running tracks in a rust-red hue, but in Paris, athletes can look forward to competing on a purple track.

Alain Blondel, sports manager in charge of athletics for Paris 2024, said the decision was a creative one.

“The big part of the job was to come up with a track that was different from what we had seen, to maintain the creative approach that the Organising Committee has had since it was set up, to go a little bit outside the box,” he said.

“The look of the Games includes three colours for all the competition venues: blue, green and purple. We decided on this purple track with different tones: lighter for the track, darker for the service areas, and grey for the turns at the end of the bend, reminding of the ash-coloured tracks that were there 100 years ago for the Olympic Games Paris 1924.”

Who else than Carl Lewis to unveil our purple track 💜The Stade de France is more than ready for the Athletics event this summer. Be ready, this track will be faaaast ⚡pic.twitter.com/cmxzDaVIzH

He added: “We had to work hard on the colours, so that they came out in the best possible tones to highlight the athletes.

“It’s a track, it has to be pretty, but above all it’s a stage on which the athletes are going to perform. What’s really important is that the colours and the athletes stand out.”

Tony Estanguet, president of Paris 2024, said this year’s Olympic aesthetic comprised elegance, creativity and the “French art de vivre”.

The purple track intends to convey a “joyful and festive tone”, he added.

It was designed by Italian company Mondo, which has been responsible for every Olympic track since 1976.

With Paris 2024 aiming to bethe most sustainable Summer Games ever, Mondo has incorporated some unusual natural materials in their track.

These include the shells of bivalve molluscs, such as clams and mussels, which contain calcium carbonate, a substance often used in resilient flooring.

The shells are cleaned and ground into a powder to use in the tracks.

Alessandro Piceli, a research and development manager at Mondo, claims that their current purple iteration is even better than that used at the 2020 Tokyo Games.

“We are focusing on the dynamic connection between the track and a new generation of shoes,” he toldThe Guardian.

“For the track, we only see the aesthetics, but there is great work that goes into the underlayer.

“A new granule of polymeric material, made especially for it, was inserted in the Tokyo track. We have made it even better now. We have chemists, engineers and physicists who take care of the performance of the material.”When it comes to running tracks, red is a popular colour as it is the most UV stable, meaning it is more resistant to fading in the sunlight.

Green and blue are also desirable choices as these are less prone to wearing and fading.

Most running tracks are made from a multilayered system composed of rubber andplastic.

Historically, they were constructed from rubber and asphalt, while some incorporated sand or even hay.

The 1960s saw the arrival of polyurethane surfaces. The Mexico City Olympics in 1968 marked the first time a synthetic system was used at an international track-and-field event.

Athletes of a certain age may also know running tracks as “tartan” – the name that US company 3M gave to the polyurethane floor it developed for the 1968 Games.

While 3M stopped making the track material decades ago, the term “tartan” endures in the sporting lexicon.

title: Why is the Paris 2024 Olympic running track purple?
author: Joanna Whitehead
description: Team GB's runners will be pounding a bright lavender circuit containing seashells - here's why
